nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Malawi, endemic to the area around Likoma Island; associated with rock piles and boulder fields in the shallow sublittoral, where it grazes aufwuchs algae from rock surfaces.

decorPreferences: Heavy rockwork with numerous caves, crevices, and sightline breaks
Aragonite or coral-sand substrate to buffer pH and kH
Open midwater swimming lanes between rock structures
Minimal or no live plants (will be uprooted or nibbled)
Rocks stacked tall enough to interrupt male-male line-of-sight

diet.notes: An aufwuchs grazer in the wild — scrapes algae mats and associated microfauna from rock surfaces. In the aquarium, spirulina-based pellets or flakes, algae wafers, and blanched vegetable matter (spinach, zucchini, peas) are generally advised as the staple. Frozen brine shrimp, daphnia, or mysis are sometimes offered in small amounts as occasional supplements, but high-protein foods (bloodworms, beef heart, tubifex) have been associated with Malawi bloat — a serious and often fatal digestive condition — and are best avoided or used very sparingly. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: Other robust Mbuna of similar size but visibly distinct coloration (e.g. Labidochromis caeruleus, Iodotropheus sprengerae, Metriaclima species)
Synodontis catfish (S. multipunctatus, S. petricola) as Lake Malawi-region bottom dwellers
Other Cynotilapia species only if from clearly distinct color forms (hybrid risk)
avoidWith: Multiple males of the same species (extreme conspecific aggression; harem of 1 male to 3–4+ females is generally advised)
Other blue-and-black-striped Mbuna (e.g. Melanochromis johannii, Pseudotropheus saulosi) — triggers heightened aggression and risks hybridisation
Peaceful Lake Malawi haps and peacock cichlids (Aulonocara) — typically outcompeted at feeding and harassed
Community fish, slow-moving species, long-finned fish

summary: The Maingano Cichlid is an electric-blue-and-black horizontally striped Mbuna endemic to Likoma Island in Lake Malawi. Highly aggressive toward males of its own kind and toward similar-looking species, it is generally kept in a harem of one male and three or more females in a large, rock-dominated aquarium.
careNotes:
The Maingano Cichlid was formally described in 1997 under the name Cynotilapia cyaneorhabdos, and the older names Pseudotropheus cyaneorhabdos and Melanochromis cyaneorhabdos are still widely encountered in the hobby. The species is endemic to the rocky shores around Likoma Island, Lake Malawi, where it grazes aufwuchs (the layer of algae and associated microfauna that coats rock surfaces) in roughly 5–10 m of water. It has been assessed as Critically Endangered by the IUCN, with the aquarium trade cited as a pressure, so sourcing captive-bred stock rather than wild-collected fish is generally advised.

Coloration is distinctive: rather than the vertical bars typical of many Mbuna, Maingano show high-contrast electric-blue and black horizontal stripes along the body. Sexes are very similar — both retain the striped pattern — but mature males tend to be slightly larger, more streamlined, and darker overall, while females often show a paler blue belly and a rounder profile. This near-monomorphism makes it difficult to build accurate sex ratios from a young group, so most keepers start with 6–8 juveniles and rehome surplus males later.

Conspecific aggression tends to be high and is directed primarily at other males of the same species and at any visually similar Mbuna, such as Melanochromis johannii and Pseudotropheus saulosi. A harem structure — one dominant male with three or four or more females — is the conventional stocking approach. Keeping multiple males in the same tank generally only works in very large setups (300 L or more) with abundant rockwork arranged to break sightlines between males. Hybridisation risk is real, so mixing with other Cynotilapia species or color forms is not recommended.

Water chemistry should reflect the firm, alkaline, mineralized profile of the Rift Lakes — pH around 7.7–8.7, moderately hard, and stable. An aragonite or coral-sand substrate and well-buffered water-change water make this easier to maintain than relying on chemical additives alone. Soft, acidic, or unstable water is harmful.

Lake Malawi cichlids tend to be sensitive to nitrate accumulation; weekly 25–30% water changes and strong mechanical and biological filtration are generally advised. A diet based on plant matter and algae protects against Malawi bloat — a serious and often fatal digestive condition — so bloodworms and other high-protein meaty foods should be kept to an occasional treat at most.

Regarding tank size: some retailers list as little as 110 L (roughly 30 US gallons) as a minimum, but successful long-term keeping of a harem alongside mixed Mbuna tankmates generally calls for a tank with a long footprint of 120 cm or more and a volume of 200 L or more, which provides enough space to absorb aggression and allow multiple territories.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder, like other Mbuna. Spawning typically occurs over a flat rock or cleared sandy patch the male has prepared and defends. The male displays vividly to ripe females and uses anal-fin egg-spot mimicry during the spawning sequence; the female picks up the just-laid eggs into her mouth and takes in milt while pecking at the male's egg-spots, completing fertilisation orally. Females hold the brood for roughly 2–3 weeks (around 12–21 days has been reported, depending on temperature) before releasing free-swimming fry. Clutch size is modest — commonly 10–30 fry. Fry accept finely crushed spirulina flake, micro-pellets, and freshly hatched brine shrimp from release. Holding females are vulnerable to harassment and may need to be stripped or moved to a separate brooding tank to maximise fry yield in a busy community.

primary.name: Wikipedia — Pseudotropheus cyaneorhabdos
primary.notes: Aquarium Co-Op product page (https://www.aquariumcoop.com/products/maingano-cichlid) returned HTTP 404, and the SeriouslyFish page for Cynotilapia sp. 'Elongatus Maingano' also returned 404. Wikipedia was used as a substitute primary source — provided taxonomy/synonymy (Pseudotropheus/Melanochromis/Cynotilapia cyaneorhabdos, Bowers & Stauffer 1997), max size 7.5 cm SL, native range (Likoma Island, Lake Malawi), depth range 5–10 m, IUCN Critically Endangered status, and the monomorphic-sex / horizontal-stripe distinction from P. johannii.

additional.name: ThatPetPlace — Electric Blue Maingano product profile
additional.notes: Retail care profile (substitute for the 404'd Aquarium Co-Op page). Listed temperature 76–80°F (24–27°C), pH 7.8–8.6, minimum tank 30 US gal (~110 L), max size 3 in (~7.6 cm), and high male-male / between-species aggression.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Maingano Cichlid care guide
additional.notes: Detailed husbandry profile. Listed adult size 3–4 in (7.5–10 cm), temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 7.6–8.8, minimum tank 40–50 US gal (150–190 L) with +20–30 gal per additional fish, lifespan 8–9 years, brooding interval ~12–14 days, sexing cues, and diet/aggression notes. Tank-size guidance was used to push the playbook's tank-size 'bias-larger' rule up to 200 L over the retail-source 110 L.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — The mbuna keeper's survival guide
additional.notes: General Mbuna husbandry — rock-dominated decor, harem stocking (1 male + multiple females), avoid mixing similar-color species, Malawi-bloat risk from high-protein diets, weekly large water changes for nitrate control. Used to corroborate husbandry guidance not specific to a single source.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; females hold the brood for roughly 12–21 days. Clutch size typically runs 10–30 fry. Fry accept finely crushed spirulina flake and freshly hatched brine shrimp immediately on release. Spawning tends to come readily in a stable harem once water chemistry is firm and alkaline; fry survival in a busy community Mbuna tank is generally low without separating the holding female or moving the fry.
